guest: Sam Ratner, founder who sold a sports-betting startup to FuboTV for ~$40M, now building Showroom

business_ideas.idea: Buy decommissioned riverboat casinos cheaply and convert them into restaurants/entertainment venues or reopen as casinos
Buy a rural vending-machine company, let Coke/Pepsi contracts expire, switch to cheap store-brand snacks to massively boost margins
National water-taxi network (buy out a local lake operator, then expand lake-town by lake-town, eventually sell to Uber/Lyft)
Buy a dominant local college-town bar/nightlife venue (like 'Shooters' or 'Shady Gators') and add daytime uses (yoga studio by day, club by night) plus modern payments to multiply revenue
A 'blue collar Thiel Fellowship' that recruits non-elite students to find and buy an overlooked local business near their campus instead of going to college
Showroom: an AI/LLM-powered vertical shopping search engine spanning thousands of merchants and brands not on Amazon (e.g., Gucci, Burberry), monetized via merchant revenue share
Hire divers to recover the reportedly millions of dollars of lost GoPros at the bottom of Lake of the Ozarks

predictions.text: Uber will acquire a water-taxi company by around 2028.
Showroom will be worth at least a billion dollars within 5 years (by end of 2028).

dynamics: Classic guest-interview format with Sam Parr leading most of the questioning and Shaan playing a lighter supporting role, occasionally riffing on ideas (the blue-collar Thiel Fellowship concept) and adding color commentary. Energy is high and playful, with recurring comic bits (the Shady Gators menu, Floribama) breaking up dense business-strategy segments. Structural note: this episode explicitly uses the recurring 'blue collar side hustle' segment framing.
frameworks: Never pitch twice: fundraising/recruiting is informative, not persuasive — genuine buy-in shows up immediately, don't chase people who need convincing
The potential you see in others is a mirror of your own ambition, not theirs — take people at face value for what they actually want
The value is in the duck calls: real profit hides in niche, overlooked, regional micro-markets most people dismiss as too small or weird
Aim for 5x improvement, not 10% improvement: only pursue deals where you can identify a path to make something dramatically better, not marginally better
learnings: Deep, hands-on due diligence (flying to remote towns, calling junkyards, talking to regulators and employees on-site) reveals opportunities that a few Google searches never would.
Owning appreciated stock and borrowing against it instead of selling lets you deploy capital for new bets while avoiding a taxable event.
Entering a heavily regulated, capital-intensive industry (like licensed gambling) can be a moat precisely because most competitors are unwilling to endure the compliance grind.
A consumer platform built around real transactions (fees, revenue share) monetizes far more reliably than one built around attention/engagement alone.
Brand identity, not literal product specs, is what differentiates otherwise nearly identical goods (e.g., two black t-shirts) — which is why AI-driven shopping search needs brand-aware training data.

banger_quotes.quote: The value is in the duck calls.
I've always been a zero backup plan guy.
If it's easy, there can't be any value in it.
Capital is pretty cheap. If you have a great business, you're going to raise the money.

numbers_dropped: Sold sports-betting startup to FuboTV for ~$40 million at age 23, before it even launched
High school snow removal business generated ~$50-60K per winter
Riverboat casino purchase price was under $1 million (7 figures)
Vending machine company he passed on: 5,000 machines, seller wanted $4-5 million, ~$1.2-1.3M annual cash flow
Lake water-taxi operator making ~$400K per boat per summer across 3 boats (~$1.2M/year)
FuboTV was doing roughly $1 billion/year in revenue (~$700M subscriptions, ~$300M advertising) when guest departed
notable: Guest is a 27-year-old repeat founder discussing an eclectic string of niche acquisitions (a decommissioned riverboat casino, a rural vending-machine operator, a lake water-taxi business) alongside his current AI shopping-search startup, framed under the show's recurring 'blue collar side hustle' segment.
